core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing black sapote are the same: full sun, free-draining soil, a warm and frost-free spot, a named grafted tree, and the discipline to pick the fruit hard and ripen it indoors. The chocolate pudding fruit is a tropical persimmon, so it rewards a leaner, drier hand than most fruit trees. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere black sapote will grow.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Black sapote is sold both as cheap unnamed seedlings and as grafted, named cultivars, and the grafted trees are the ones worth seeking out because they fruit far sooner and to a known size and seediness. The varieties grafted and sold in Australia include Maher (very large, flattened, few-seeded fruit on an unusually small and prolific tree to about 4 metres, which makes it the pick for a backyard), Bernecker (near-spherical fruit with only one or two seeds and superb eating quality), Mossman (the largest fruit of all, high in pulp and grown more for processing than dessert), Superb (a heavy-bearing North Queensland selection of small fruit that is often completely seedless), Ricks Late (which carries its crop later in the season) and Colossal (a very large, self-pollinating selection). You will also see Tahiti and Cocktail offered. Pick a named one and let the in-stock table above show what is available right now.

sections.heading: Seedless or seeded? It depends on pollination
sections.body: Whether your tree bears seedless fruit comes down to its flowers and what is growing nearby. Many seedlings and self-pollinating selections such as Colossal carry heavy crops, but the fruit holds one to five seeds. Some prized selections have female-only flowers and set seedless fruit when no compatible pollen reaches them, yet the very same tree will set a heavier crop of seeded fruit if a seedling or perfect-flowered tree blooms close by. So a pollinator lifts your yield but costs you the seedlessness. If seedless fruit is the goal, grow a known female-flowered selection (Superb is the usual choice) on its own, away from other black sapotes; if sheer quantity matters more, plant a pollinator alongside and accept the seeds.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Black sapote wants full sun and free-draining soil. It grows on a wide range of soils, does best on a moist sandy loam, and will even shrug off short spells of flooding, but it resents permanent waterlogging, so avoid low spots where water sits and plant on a low mound if your ground is heavy. A slightly acid to neutral soil of about pH 6 to 7 suits it. The tree is slow for its first three or four years, putting on perhaps 30 centimetres a year, then speeds up, so give a full-size tree 8 to 10 metres of room (less for compact Maher). Young trees are frost-tender and want a warm, sheltered position; mulch well but keep the mulch off the trunk. Container-grown trees go in best in the warm months (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water, feeding, and the shy-bearer trap
sections.body: The commonest mistake with black sapote is kindness. A tree that is heavily fed and watered runs to lush leaf and bears shyly, while the heaviest croppers are often the half-neglected ones on leaner, drier ground. Water a young tree regularly to establish it, and irrigate any bearing tree through a real dry spell, but otherwise hold back. A simple, long-used routine is to lime, fertilise and lightly mulch once a year after harvest and then largely leave the tree alone, keeping soil moisture up only in severe dry weather. Go easy on nitrogen in particular, since it is what drives the leafy, fruitless growth. A leaner regime also gives smaller but noticeably richer fruit.

sections.heading: Harvest: the calyx test, and never ripen on the tree
sections.body: Black sapote is picked hard and green and ripened indoors, never left to ripen on the tree. The signal that a fruit is mature and ready to pick is the calyx, the green cap on top: while the fruit is filling, the cap sits tight against it, and when the cap loosens and its edges lift away from the fruit all round, that fruit is ready. The glossy bright-green skin also dulls toward olive and then a muddy green. Cut the fruit from the tree, never pull it, and lay the fruit out to soften over about three to six days at room temperature. Do not wait for soft fruit on the branch: a ripe black sapote is jelly-soft and splatters messily if it falls, and a tree-ripened fruit rarely comes good.

sections.heading: Eating it, and the unripe warning
sections.body: A black sapote is ready to eat only when it is completely soft, giving all over like an overripe persimmon and holding the dent of a finger. Cut it across the middle, lift out the few flat seeds, and scoop the dark, glossy pulp, which eats like a mild, sweet chocolate mousse and is lovely fresh with a squeeze of lime, with cream, or with a little rum or coffee liqueur; it also freezes well and bakes into cakes and ice cream. The warning matters: an unripe black sapote is not merely disappointing but astringent, caustic, bitter and an irritant, the same mouth-puckering tannin that marks an unripe persimmon, so wait for full softness and never serve it firm.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted black sapote rather than an unnamed seedling. A grafted tree fruits in about two years against five to seven for a seedling, comes true to a known fruit, and lets you match the variety to your plot, a compact Maher for a small garden or a big-fruited Mossman for the kitchen. Check the label for the cultivar name and, if seedless fruit matters to you, for whether it should be grown away from a pollinator. Good black sapote is not always easy to find and the grafted, named trees sell out, which is exactly why the in-stock table above is worth watching.

faqs.q: What does black sapote taste like?
faqs.a: Ripe black sapote is mild, sweet and low in acid, with a smooth texture often compared to chocolate mousse or a soft caramel custard, which is how it earned the name chocolate pudding fruit. It does not actually taste of cocoa; the appeal is the rich, pudding-like flesh rather than a strong flavour, and many people lift it with a squeeze of citrus.

Yes, in the sense that it belongs to the same genus, Diospyros, as the orange persimmon, which is why some growers call it the black or chocolate persimmon. It is an evergreen, tropical member of that family rather than the deciduous, cool-climate kind, and it shares the persimmon habit of being harshly astringent until it is fully ripe.

faqs.q: Can I grow black sapote in a pot?
faqs.a: You can grow a compact grafted variety such as Maher in a large container for some years, which suits a courtyard or a frost-prone climate where you need to move it under cover in winter. Use a free-draining mix, water through the warm months because pots dry out fast, feed lightly so it does not run to leaf, and pot it up every couple of years.

faqs.q: How big does a black sapote tree get, and can I keep it small?
faqs.a: In ideal tropics the tree can reach 10 metres or more, but in most Australian gardens it stays smaller, and the grafted Maher selection naturally tops out at around 4 metres, which is why it is the usual choice where space is tight. Any black sapote also takes pruning after harvest to hold it to a height you can net and pick, so size need not put you off.

WA.intro: Growing black sapote in Western Australia. WA can grow it, but only in the warm tropical north, not the cool Mediterranean south-west, so in Perth it is a sheltered-microclimate gamble rather than a sure thing.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Black sapote is a tropical tree that wants warmth and freedom from frost. The cool, wet winters and relatively short summer heat of the south-west are its main obstacle in Western Australia, so flowering and ripening there are unreliable, even though an established tree would rarely be killed by the mild winter cold. The tree belongs in WA's hot north, where the heat and a real dry season suit it.

sections.heading: Where black sapote grows in WA
sections.body: WA's tropical and subtropical north is the place for it: Kununurra and the Ord River irrigation area in the east Kimberley, Broome, and Carnarvon on the Gascoyne coast all grow a range of tropical fruit. Black sapote has been grown and tasted as far south as Perth and around Carnarvon by WA enthusiasts, but in the south-west it is strictly a trial for the hottest, most sheltered, frost-free corner of the garden.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Black sapote is as trouble-free in the west as anywhere, with scale and its sooty mould the only routine worry. The fruit-fly story is different here, though: Western Australia's pest is the Mediterranean fruit fly, not the Queensland fruit fly, and it is established across the state and will sting soft ripe fruit, so pick black sapote hard and ripen it indoors out of its reach.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: Western Australia's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must meet import conditions on the Western Australian Organism List, and many eastern nurseries will not post trees here, so the in-stock table above is filtered to the sellers that can ship to WA. In practice that is mostly WA nurseries plus the few interstate sellers that meet the rules.

faqs.q: Can you grow chocolate pudding fruit in Perth?
faqs.a: You can try one, but treat it as a long shot. The chocolate pudding fruit needs steady warmth to set and fill its crop, and the south-west's mild, damp winters tend to leave it sulking and shy, so plant it in your hottest, driest, most protected corner and regard any fruit as a bonus. The reliable WA growing country is up north.

faqs.q: Will black sapote survive a Perth winter?
faqs.a: Usually yes, but surviving and fruiting are not the same thing. The mild winter cold of the south-west rarely kills an established tree, since it can take a brief dip to about minus 2 degrees; the trouble is the lack of sustained heat, which leaves the tree healthy but reluctant to flower and ripen. Sharp drainage and a hot, sheltered wall give it the best chance.

QLD.intro: Growing black sapote in Queensland. Queensland is the home of black sapote in Australia: the warm, frost-free tropical north grows it best, and because an established tree shrugs off the odd light frost, it also fruits down through the subtropical south-east where fussier tropical fruits will not.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Black sapote belongs in the warm, humid, frost-free tropics, which is why far-north Queensland grows it so well. It is also one of the hardier rare tropical fruits: an established tree has taken brief cold down to about minus 1 or minus 2 degrees, so unlike mangosteen or durian it will crop in the cooler subtropical south-east as well, given a warm, sheltered spot. Young trees, though, are frost-tender and need protection through their first winters.

sections.heading: Where black sapote grows in QLD
sections.body: The wet tropics coast around Cairns, Innisfail, Babinda, Tully and Mossman, and the Atherton Tableland and Mareeba on the range behind them, is the heartland, and the rare-fruit growers around Kuranda were among the first to fruit it here. It crops north to the Daintree, through the Mackay and Whitsunday sugar country, and on down into the warmer parts of the subtropical south-east, including the Sunshine Coast hinterland and sheltered Brisbane gardens.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: The main Queensland harvest runs from late winter through spring and into early summer, roughly August to December, though in the warmest tropical districts a healthy tree can carry and ripen fruit over a much longer spread, close to year-round at the top of the state. Pick the fruit hard, as the cap lifts, and ripen it indoors over a few days.

sections.heading: The shy-bearer trap on rich coastal soils
sections.body: Queensland growers learned early that a black sapote on rich, well-watered coastal soil often grows into a magnificent leafy tree that sets little fruit. The reliable croppers are frequently the trees on poorer, drier, sandy or sloping ground, or in a spot that gets a genuine dry season. If your tree is all leaf and no fruit, ease right off the water and nitrogen once it is established and let it harden.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Black sapote is one of the most trouble-free fruit trees you can grow in Queensland. Scale insects, and the sooty mould that grows on their honeydew, are the main thing to watch and are easily knocked back with a horticultural oil. Importantly, black sapote is not a listed Queensland fruit fly host, and because the fruit is picked hard and ripened indoors it largely sidesteps fruit fly anyway, though ripe windfalls will still draw possums, flying foxes and birds.

faqs.q: Can you grow black sapote in Brisbane or the Sunshine Coast?
faqs.a: Yes, and more reliably than you might expect. The chocolate pudding fruit copes with the occasional light frost of the subtropical south-east once it has a few years on it, so gardeners there crop it in a protected, sunny corner long after a mangosteen or rambutan would have failed. The main job is to nurse it through its first couple of winters.

faqs.q: Which black sapote is best for a Queensland backyard?
faqs.a: For a single tree in a suburban yard the grafted Maher is the standout: it is naturally small, around 4 metres, yet a prolific bearer of large, few-seeded fruit. If you have room and want sheer volume for the kitchen, the big-fruited Mossman or a self-pollinating Colossal will keep you in pudding for months.

NSW.intro: Growing black sapote in New South Wales. NSW is the warm southern edge of dependable black sapote country: the frost-free far north coast ripens it well, and because the tree takes a light frost once established, sheltered gardens a good way further south can grow it too.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Black sapote needs warmth and freedom from hard frost. Young trees are killed by even a light frost, so in New South Wales the safe ground is the frost-free far north coast. Established trees are tougher, taking brief cold to around minus 2 degrees, which is why the species has fruited in surprisingly cool spots, including sheltered Sydney and Newcastle gardens, wherever a warm aspect and good drainage take the edge off winter.

sections.heading: Where black sapote grows in NSW
sections.body: The Northern Rivers, the warm coastal valleys of the Tweed, Brunswick, Richmond and Clarence around Murwillumbah, Lismore, Byron Bay and Grafton, is the heartland, and several of Australia's best black sapotes were bred here, including the late-season Ricks Late and selections from Kyogle and Newrybar. South of there the tree becomes a sheltered-garden proposition, though warm coastal pockets down to the Coffs Harbour district and beyond can ripen a crop.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Cooler than the tropics, the New South Wales crop is later and more concentrated, running mainly through spring and into early summer. The locally bred late selections such as Ricks Late were chosen precisely to stretch the season on, carrying fruit into late spring; a warm position helps the fruit fill and soften before the cool weather closes in.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Black sapote stays refreshingly easy in New South Wales: scale and the sooty mould that follows it are the main bother, and a winter oil spray handles them. Queensland fruit fly is endemic along the warm NSW coast, but black sapote is not one of its favoured hosts and is picked hard, so it is far less fruit-fly-prone than most backyard fruit; net or bag any fruit you allow to soften, and expect birds and flying foxes to take an interest in windfalls.

faqs.q: Can you grow black sapote in Sydney?
faqs.a: It is a gamble worth taking in the right spot. Sydney is cooler and more frost-prone than black sapote likes, but mature trees have fruited there in warm, frost-free pockets against a sunny north-facing wall with sharp drainage. Treat it as a microclimate experiment, start with a hardy grafted tree or a movable pot, and count any fruit as a win.

faqs.q: Which variety should I plant on the far north coast?
faqs.a: You are spoiled for choice, since the district bred several of the best. For a late crop choose Ricks Late, for sheer size the Colossal, and for dependable all-round fruit the small-treed Maher or the one-or-two-seeded Bernecker. Buy a grafted tree rather than a seedling so you know exactly what you are getting.

VIC.intro: Growing black sapote in Victoria. Black sapote is not a Victorian outdoor crop: the state is too cold and too frosty for a tropical persimmon to flower and ripen, so here it is a glasshouse or large-pot curiosity rather than a backyard fruit tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Black sapote wants tropical to warm-subtropical conditions and will not take a hard frost: young trees are killed by a light frost of about minus 1 degree, and even mature trees only tolerate brief cold near minus 2 degrees. Melbourne sits well below that through winter, with overnight minimums averaging around 6 degrees and ground frosts common, and the inland and ranges are colder again, so the tree cannot get the frost-free warmth it needs to fruit.

sections.heading: Growing black sapote in Victoria anyway
sections.body: If you want to try, grow it as a container plant: a compact grafted Maher in a large pot, kept in the warmest, most sheltered corner of the garden, or better still in a heated glasshouse or conservatory, and moved under cover for winter. Expect handsome evergreen foliage far more often than fruit. For a crop you can count on, a hardier subtropical fruit is the wiser choice, and black sapote is best enjoyed bought as fruit from the northern harvest.

faqs.q: Will a black sapote fruit outdoors in Melbourne?
faqs.a: No. A tropical persimmon needs a long, warm, frost-free season to flower and ripen, and Melbourne's cold, frosty winters do not provide it. A potted tree kept under glass may survive for years, but it will almost never set and ripen a usable crop outdoors in the Victorian climate.

faqs.q: Is there a cold-hardy black sapote for a cool climate?
faqs.a: No. No selection takes hard frost, though the grafted Maher is at least compact enough to live in a pot you can shelter. If you are set on a chocolate-textured, persimmon-style fruit in a cooler climate, the deciduous astringent persimmon is the tree that actually belongs there; black sapote stays a warm-climate plant.
